title: Vacation Rental Turnover Cleaning Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Vacation Rental Cleaning Business: Costs and Earnings
metaDescription: Honest guide to a vacation rental turnover cleaning business: real startup costs, per-turnover earnings, finding Airbnb hosts, and what people get wrong.
bestFor: Reliable, fast, detail-oriented people who can hit tight same-day windows and want recurring hands-on work
biggestRisk: Missing a tight same-day checkout-to-checkin window, leaving a host with a dirty property and a furious guest
whatItIs: A vacation rental turnover cleaning business cleans and resets short-term rentals, Airbnb, Vrbo, and other STR properties, between guests. It is distinct from regular house cleaning because the work is hotel-style: you clean, fully reset the space, change and launder linens, restock supplies and amenities, stage the property to match listing photos, and report any damage or missing items, all within a tight window between one guest's checkout and the next guest's check-in (often 11am to 3pm the same day). It is also distinct from full short-term rental management, which handles bookings, pricing, and guest communication, here you focus on the physical turnover. The appeal is recurring, predictable per-turnover income tied to a host's booking calendar.
dailyReality: A turnover means arriving after checkout and working fast through a checklist: strip and remake beds with fresh linens, clean bathrooms and kitchen, vacuum and mop, take out trash, restock toilet paper, coffee, soap, and welcome items, stage the space exactly like the listing photos, and photograph the finished result and any damage. Speed and consistency matter intensely because the next guest may arrive the same afternoon. Most turnovers take one to three hours depending on size, and busy operators chain several same-day turnovers in a tight route. Weekends, holidays, and check-out days are the busiest, and last-minute schedule changes from hosts are constant.

startupCostsItemized.item: Cleaning supplies, caddy, microfiber cloths, mop, vacuum
Backup linen and towel sets (so you can swap, not wait on laundry)
Restock starter inventory (toilet paper, soap, coffee, trash bags)
Smartphone with checklist/turnover app and a camera
Vehicle for hauling supplies and linens (or one you own)
Google Business Profile + simple website and photos

realEarnings.yearOne: Solo beginners typically earn $1,500 to $3,500 per month part-time, charging a flat fee per turnover (commonly $75 to $200+ depending on property size and market). Operators who lock in a few recurring properties and cluster them geographically can reach $3,000 to $5,000 per month.
realEarnings.experienced: Experienced operators with a roster of regular host clients and efficient routes commonly report $5,000 to $9,000 per month solo or with one helper, especially in tourist-heavy or high-cost markets where per-turnover fees are higher. Reliability during peak season is what keeps these clients.
realEarnings.topEarners: Top operators run cleaning teams covering dozens of properties, handle linen service in-house, and may add light maintenance and restocking management, grossing $15,000 to $50,000+ per month. Getting there requires hiring and managing reliable cleaners, scheduling software, and tight quality control across many same-day turnovers.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Geographic density (clustering properties so you can chain same-day turnovers) and reliability during peak booking periods matter most. Market matters too, tourist destinations and high-cost cities support far higher per-turnover fees than rural areas.
realEarnings.perHour: Per-turnover fees often work out to roughly $40 to $80 per hour of cleaning for an efficient solo operator, before driving and laundry time. Counting unpaid drive time, restocking runs, and laundry, realistic blended rates are commonly $30 to $55 per hour solo.

howToStart: Week 1: Buy a solid set of cleaning supplies and a caddy, and build a detailed turnover checklist (beds, baths, kitchen, restock, staging, photos). Practice a full hotel-style reset on your own home so you can do it fast and consistently. Get general liability insurance covering damage in hosts' properties before paid work.

Week 2: Set flat per-turnover pricing by property size and market, and decide whether you will handle linens (swap-and-launder) or use the host's on-site linens. Create a Google Business Profile and take photos of a clean, staged practice space.

Weeks 2-3: Reach out directly to local Airbnb and Vrbo hosts, post in STR host Facebook groups, and contact short-term rental management companies that outsource cleaning. Offer to be a reliable backup cleaner first, it is the easiest way in.

Month 1: Take your first turnovers, nail the same-day window every time, and send hosts finished photos and damage reports. Ask satisfied hosts for reviews and referrals to other hosts, who all know each other locally.

Months 1-3: Cluster properties geographically so you can chain same-day turnovers, set up reliable backup coverage for peak weekends, and tighten your checklist and restocking system so quality stays consistent as volume grows.

mustHave: Speed and consistency, hitting tight same-day checkout-to-checkin windows every time
Reliability, because a missed turnover means a dirty property and a guest complaint for the host
Attention to detail and willingness to follow a strict reset and staging checklist
canLearn: Hotel-style staging that matches a listing's photos
Efficient linen swap-and-launder systems and restocking workflows
Using turnover/checklist apps and coordinating with host calendars
separators: Rock-solid reliability during peak weekends and holidays, when hosts most need to trust their cleaner
Clustering properties geographically to chain same-day turnovers and raise the effective hourly rate
Proactive damage reporting and photo documentation that hosts come to depend on

whatPeopleGetWrong: Treating it like leisurely house cleaning and missing the tight same-day window, which damages the host's guest reviews
Taking properties scattered across town so drive time between same-day turnovers makes the schedule impossible
Forgetting restocking and staging to match listing photos, which generates guest complaints and bad host reviews
Having no backup cleaner, then leaving hosts stranded on a busy weekend after one sick day
Underpricing flat per-turnover fees without accounting for laundry, restocking runs, and supplies
Not documenting damage with photos, leaving hosts unable to claim against guests and blaming the cleaner

toolsAndEquipment.item: Cleaning supplies and a portable caddy
toolsAndEquipment.note: Carry a consistent, fully stocked kit so every turnover is identical and fast.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Quality vacuum, mop, and microfiber cloths
toolsAndEquipment.note: Reliable equipment keeps turnover times short; cheap gear slows you down.
Swapping in clean sets and laundering offsite beats waiting on a dryer mid-turnover.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Restock inventory (paper goods, toiletries, coffee, supplies)
toolsAndEquipment.note: Hosts expect amenities replenished every turnover; track and bill these.
For host calendars, checklists, and sending finished and damage photos.
You haul supplies and laundry between properties; no shop needed.

methods: Contacting local Airbnb and Vrbo hosts directly and offering reliable turnover service
Short-term rental host Facebook groups and local STR meetups, where hosts trade cleaner recommendations
Partnering with short-term rental management companies that outsource the physical cleaning
Offering to be a backup or overflow cleaner first to prove reliability before becoming primary
Referrals from existing hosts, who all know each other locally and share trusted cleaners
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Short-term rental hosts and STR management companies, concentrated in tourist destinations, vacation areas, and high-traffic cities. Hosts cluster in the same neighborhoods and talk to each other constantly, so one happy client often leads to several more.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: First turnovers usually come within one to three weeks of reaching out to hosts and management companies. Because hosts share recommendations, a reliable cleaner can build a steady roster within one to three months, reliability is rewarded fast in this niche.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Broad consumer advertising aimed at homeowners misses the mark, this is a host-to-host, B2B-style network. A fancy website before you have host reviews and finished-turnover photos does little compared to proving you never miss a window.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes. A solo operator can reach full-time income by stacking recurring properties in a tight area, especially in tourist markets with higher per-turnover fees. The solo ceiling is set by how many same-day turnovers you can physically complete in the checkout-to-checkin window.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: A clear growth path. Hiring and training cleaners lets you cover dozens of properties and peak weekends, but you take on scheduling, quality control across same-day windows, payroll, and the constant challenge of reliable backup coverage. Stepping back requires tight checklists and a dependable lead cleaner.
howItScales.sellable: A turnover business with recurring host contracts, documented systems, trained cleaners, and a brand has real resale value, often to STR management companies. A pure solo operation is closer to a job and harder to sell.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Geographic density of properties, reliable trained cleaners, scheduling and checklist software, in-house or partnered linen service, robust backup coverage for peak periods, and relationships with hosts and management companies for steady volume.

strongFit: You are fast, consistent, and can reliably hit tight same-day windows
You want recurring per-turnover income tied to a host's booking calendar
You live near a tourist destination or active short-term rental market
You are comfortable following a strict reset, staging, and documentation checklist
poorFit: You work slowly or dislike strict deadlines and last-minute schedule changes
You cannot work weekends and holidays, which are the busiest turnover days
You have no plan for backup coverage when you are sick or unavailable
You want fully passive income with no hands-on cleaning
askYourself: Can I reliably complete turnovers in the same-day window, even on busy holiday weekends?
Are there enough short-term rentals clustered near me to build an efficient route?
Who covers a turnover if I get sick on a peak weekend?

faqs.question: How is this different from regular house cleaning?
faqs.answer: Turnover cleaning is hotel-style and deadline-driven: you fully reset the space, change and launder linens, restock amenities, stage it to match the listing photos, and document damage, all within a tight checkout-to-checkin window, often the same day. Regular house cleaning is for residents on a flexible schedule. The premium and the pressure both come from the same-day timing and guest-ready standard.

faqs.question: Isn't this the same as short-term rental management?
faqs.answer: No. Short-term rental management handles bookings, pricing, guest communication, and overall operations, often for a percentage of revenue. Turnover cleaning focuses on the physical reset between guests for a flat per-turnover fee. Many cleaners work for or alongside management companies rather than competing with them.

faqs.question: How much can I charge per turnover?
faqs.answer: Flat per-turnover fees commonly range from about $75 to $200+ depending on property size, market, and whether you handle linens and restocking. Tourist destinations and high-cost cities support higher fees. Pricing flat per turnover (not hourly) aligns with how hosts budget and rewards you for being fast and efficient.

faqs.question: Do I need experience to start?
faqs.answer: No prior professional experience is required, the skills are learnable, and many operators start with no cleaning background. What you do need is speed, consistency, and reliability, since the same-day window leaves no room for slow or sloppy work. A detailed checklist and practice are how beginners get up to professional standard.

faqs.question: What happens if I miss a turnover window?
faqs.answer: It is the worst-case failure: the next guest may arrive to a dirty property, the host gets a bad review, and you likely lose the client. This is why reliability and backup coverage matter more than anything else in this business. Hosts will forgive a higher price far sooner than a missed turnover.

faqs.question: Should I handle the laundry and linens?
faqs.answer: It depends on your model. Some operators swap in fresh linen sets and launder offsite, which keeps turnovers fast and adds a billable service; others use the host's on-site machines, which is slower and can blow the same-day window. Carrying backup linen sets is the more scalable approach as you take on more properties.

faqs.question: Can I do this part-time around a job?
faqs.answer: Yes, especially if you focus on a few properties and the turnover days line up with your availability. The catch is that checkouts cluster on weekends and around midday, which may conflict with a traditional job. Many people start part-time with two or three properties and expand as their schedule and host roster allow.

dataSources: Airbnb and Vrbo host resources on turnover cleaning expectations and fees
Turnover and short-term rental cleaning platform data (Turno/TurnoverBnB pricing benchmarks)
U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, Maids and Housekeeping Cleaners (wage and employment data)
Short-term rental host communities and operator discussions for real-world per-turnover pricing and reliability norms
